turns.text: Hello Angelyn, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s hard to even know where to start. Honestly, sitting here, I feel like I’m holding my breath waiting for the other shoe to drop. My head is pounding again—that same blurring in my vision that keeps me up at night. I know the doctors call it "eclampsia," like it’s just a label on a chart, but to me, it feels like my body is turning against me, and I’m terrified I won’t be able to get to the hospital in time. I don't have a car, and the bus in Monson doesn't run on the schedule of a medical emergency. 

When I’m in that clinic, I feel like I’m standing behind a glass wall. They look at my chart—my "medium risk" status, my "never married" label—and they don’t see me. They see a puzzle to solve or a stereotype to manage. Being a multiracial woman, I feel the weight of history every time I walk into those sterile, cold rooms. I’m scared to say too much, you know? If I speak up about the pain or how scared I am, I’m worried they’ll just tag me as "difficult." I need them to see that I’m a person, not just a set of symptoms. I’m fighting for my daughter, but god, it feels like I’m fighting a whole system that never planned for us to make it out okay.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

It hits me like a slap in the face, even if it happens quietly. I spent so much time—between shifts at work, when the house was quiet and my head wasn't throbbing—writing down what I needed. I wanted to feel like I had some say in how my daughter enters this world, because everything else feels so far out of my control. 

When she just sweeps that paper aside, it confirms my worst fear: my voice is invisible here. It tells me that my preferences, my anxieties, and my dignity are secondary to their "routine." It’s like I’m just a body on a bed, a piece of equipment to be managed, rather than a mother whose life is currently hanging by a thread. I start to wonder if they even read the part about how scared I am, or if they just saw "never married" and "multiracial" and decided they already knew what kind of patient I’d be. It makes me clam up. If she can’t even look at my wishes, how can I trust her to hear me when I’m actually in pain or something goes wrong? I end up feeling even more stranded in that room, just another specimen in a system that doesn’t value me.
What had you put into that birth plan — and what had you hoped it would communicate about you, beyond the practical preferences?

I put a lot of myself into that paper. It wasn't just about medicine or the "how-to" of delivery; it was a plea for humanity. I wrote about how important it is for me to have someone explain what they’re doing *before* they do it, because I’m so tired of feeling like I’m being handled instead of cared for. I asked for a quiet room, for the lights to be dimmed, and for them to speak to me—not just over me to the other staff like I’m a ghost.

But more than the logistics, I wanted that plan to communicate that I am an equal partner in this. I wanted them to know that while I’m navigating this "medium risk" label, I am a mother who is doing everything in her power to keep her child safe despite having no car, no family nearby, and a system that keeps me on edge. I wanted them to see my strength, not just my vulnerability. When I wrote those words, I was hoping to claim some space for my daughter, to prove that her life matters as much as anyone else's. I wanted them to see the person behind the "never married" checkmark. I just wanted to be seen, truly seen, before the chaos of birth took over.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

My heart sinks because, in that moment, it feels like I’m failing a test I didn’t even realize I was taking. When she suggests the epidural, my first instinct is to wonder if she’s suggesting it because *she* is uncomfortable with my pain, or if she’s just trying to move things along so she can get to the next room. I feel that familiar, sharp prick of defensiveness—I want to prove I can do this, that my body isn't "broken" or "difficult," like the system seems to imply. 

But then there’s the exhaustion, and that terrifying memory of the eclampsia. Every time a contraction hits, that blurring in my vision gets worse, and the panic starts to claw at my throat. I start to weigh my autonomy against my survival. If I say "no," am I being strong, or am I just setting myself up to be labeled "difficult" if I end up needing help later? 

It’s a lonely place to be. I’m looking at her, trying to read if she’s an ally or just another gatekeeper, and I realize I don't have the energy to argue. I just want to survive this. I want to hold my daughter. If taking the epidural is what it takes to stop the room from spinning and keep me present enough to meet her, then maybe I have to take it—not because I failed, but because I’m trying to stay alive.
In that moment of intense pain, how clear-headed did you feel in making that decision — and do you feel you had what you needed to make a genuinely free choice?

Clear-headed? God, no. I felt like I was drowning in my own body. The pain was like a tidal wave, and between the contractions and the terrifying, shimmery edges of my vision, everything was just… blurred. When she asked, my brain felt like it was moving through thick mud. I wasn’t really making a "choice" in the way people usually mean; I was just trying to stop the spinning.

To be truly free, you have to feel safe, and I didn't feel safe. I didn't feel like I could ask, "What are the risks for me, specifically, given my blood pressure?" without being met with an eye-roll or a condescending tone. I felt like I was being nudged, pushed toward the path of least resistance for *them*. 

I didn't have what I needed. I needed a midwife to sit down, look me in the eye, and treat me like a person who is smart enough to understand the trade-offs. I needed to know that if I said "not yet," I wouldn't be punished for it. Instead, I felt cornered. I made the choice because I was scared of the pain, yes, but I was even more scared of what would happen if I didn't cooperate. That’s not freedom. That’s just survival.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

When they said "emergency," the whole world just narrowed down to the sound of my own heart beating in my ears. The silence of my apartment, the long bus rides to the clinic—all of that felt like a lifetime ago. Suddenly, there were hands everywhere, people rushing in and out, and nobody was looking at me; they were looking at monitors and shouting numbers I couldn’t quite process. 

It felt violent, the way my agency was stripped away in those seconds. One minute I was trying to breathe through the contractions, and the next, I was being moved toward a surgery I hadn’t prepared for. I remember thinking, *Is this how it ends? Is this the moment they decide for me?* There was this overwhelming fear that because I was already a "medium risk" statistic, they were just going to do what was fastest for them, not necessarily what was safest for me. 

I felt like an object being transferred from one station to the next. I wanted to scream, "Wait, tell me what you're doing!" but the room felt too crowded with their authority. I just closed my eyes and tried to hold onto the thought of my daughter, praying that she’d be okay, even while I felt completely discarded in the process. I was terrified that I was disappearing.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

When they asked my companion to leave, the air in the room seemed to go thin. That silence I’m so used to at home suddenly felt like a threat. Without my person there to hold my hand or anchor me to the room, I felt completely exposed—like a subject being prepped for an experiment rather than a mother. 

The staff moving around me were efficient, sure, but their efficiency felt cold. They were talking about me—using words like "c-section prep" and "maternal vitals"—but they weren't talking *to* me. I felt like I was being dismantled piece by piece. My biggest fear in those minutes wasn’t the surgery; it was the total erasure of my humanity. 

What I needed? I didn't need another medical explanation. I needed someone to look at me, truly see the terror in my eyes, and just say, "I’m here, you’re safe, and I’m going to stay with you until this is done." I needed them to recognize that I’m a human being, not just a procedure on a checklist. I needed them to acknowledge that being left alone in that state is a form of violence. I just needed to know that I mattered enough to them for them to treat me like a person, not a problem.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

It’s funny, the difference between "doing their job" and actually being there. When my companion was gone, the staff shifted into this mode where they just seemed to stop talking to me entirely. They’d announce things to each other, like, "BP is elevated, let’s get the drape up," or "Starting the prep now." It was like I had suddenly turned invisible. They weren't mean, exactly, but they were *clinical* in a way that felt like they were scrubbing the humanity out of the room to get to the biology.

That made the absence of my person feel like a massive, gaping hole. If someone had just taken a second—just a second—to put a hand on my shoulder or ask, "Angelyn, are you with us? I know this is scary, but we’re right here," it would have changed everything. Instead, the sterile, detached efficiency made me feel like I was just a carcass being processed. Every time they touched me to adjust a lead or check a monitor without telling me first, it felt like an intrusion. They weren't compensating for his absence at all; they were amplifying it. They were treating me like a project they were rushing to finish, and in that vacuum, all the fear about being "unfit" or "difficult" just rushed back in. I felt small, and I felt entirely alone in a room full of people.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

The first few minutes after they pulled her out are just a blur of noise and white light. I was so exhausted, my body felt like lead, and the fear that had been gripping my chest hadn't quite let go yet. When I heard her cry, it wasn't the relief people talk about in movies; it was more like a shock to my system. I was waiting for someone to hand her to me, to validate that we both actually made it through the fire, but there was this strange delay where they were busy with their paperwork and their final checks.

In that half-hour, I felt like a spectator to my own life. They were moving her around, and I was just lying there, still feeling the echo of the surgery, desperate to just pull her against my skin and know she was real. I kept watching the staff, waiting for one of them to smile at me, to acknowledge that I was a mother who had just done something monumental, but they were still in "clinical mode." 

I felt this hollow ache—a mix of joy that she was here and a deep, simmering anger that the system had stolen the start of our story. I wanted to be in a bubble, just the two of us, but instead, I was still stuck in that cold room, surrounded by people who were already moving on to the next patient. I held her, finally, and I made a promise to her right then: I would fight to keep her safe from this world, no matter what it takes.
Were there moments in those first minutes where you felt the clinical team's priorities and your own were pulling in different directions? What happened?

Oh, absolutely. The second she was out, all I wanted was to hold her—to feel her skin against mine, to just *be* with her. That was my priority; my brain was screaming for that connection, for that proof that we had survived. But for the staff, the priorities were somewhere else entirely. 

They were so focused on the logistics—weighing her, doing the Apgar scores, checking my vitals, all the "post-op" protocol. They kept saying, "We just need to finish this, then you can have her." Every time they said that, it felt like they were putting a wall between us. They were working on a clock I didn't care about. They were prioritizing the data—the numbers on their charts—over the human need for bonding. 

It felt like they didn't trust that I could handle her, or maybe they just didn't think the connection mattered as much as the documentation. When I tried to reach for her, I felt like they were gently, but firmly, pushing me back. It made me feel like an outsider in my own birth experience. They weren't bad people, I know that, but they were so trapped in their own clinical priorities that they completely missed the fact that my daughter and I needed to reconnect to prove we were still here. They were processing a birth; I was trying to mother my child. We were in two different worlds.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

My deepest, most paralyzing fear was that the doctors would look at my life—the fact that I'm not married, that I have no car, that I live in a quiet, lonely apartment—and decide that I wasn't a capable mother. I was terrified that they would see me as "disposable" or "unfit" and that they would make decisions *for* me rather than *with* me. I was so scared that when the crisis hit, they’d treat my daughter as a prize they were rescuing from an unsuitable parent, rather than my child.

When I was in the thick of it, that fear manifested every time they stopped talking to me and started talking over me. It felt like they were slowly taking ownership of my body and my baby. 

What helped? Honestly, very little, but there was one moment when a nurse—she was newer, maybe younger—stopped, looked me directly in the eyes, and actually said, "Angelyn, I see you’re scared. You’re doing the work of a warrior right now." It wasn't about the medicine. It was just her acknowledging that I was there and that I was capable. That one sentence shattered the glass wall for a second. It reminded me that I wasn't just a chart; I was a mother fighting for her life. It gave me the tiny bit of strength I needed to keep going.

Imagine it's your first night at home with your newborn after being discharged from hospital. You're exhausted, possibly in pain, and your baby won't settle. What does that night feel like, and who — if anyone — is there with you?

It’s quiet—a heavy, hollow quiet that feels like it’s pressing against the walls of this small apartment. The hospital was noisy, but at least there were people. Here, it’s just the hum of the refrigerator and the high-pitched, desperate cry of my baby. My incision burns with every movement, a sharp, angry reminder of how my birth didn't go the way I imagined. I’m physically drained, my eyes are gritty, and the exhaustion is so deep it feels like it’s settled into my bones.

I’m alone. My phone sits on the counter, silent. There’s no one to hand her to, no one to offer me a glass of water or just sit in the room while I try to figure out how to calm her down. Every time she cries, a fresh wave of panic hits me. *Am I doing this right? Is she hungry, or is she sick, or is she sensing that her mother is terrified?* I start second-guessing every move. The "medium risk" label the doctors gave me keeps looping in my head, and I’m terrified that if I don’t get this perfect, someone is going to show up and tell me I wasn't cut out for this. It’s just me and this tiny life, trying to survive the night in a world that feels like it’s waiting for me to trip.
When you were discharged, how prepared did you feel for what was actually waiting for you at home — and what was the biggest gap between what you expected and what you found?

I left that hospital feeling like I’d been pushed out of an airlock. They handed me a stack of papers with instructions about wound care and signs of infection, but those pages felt paper-thin against the reality of the silence in my apartment. I felt like I was being sent back into the arena, just with a much heavier burden than I started with.

The biggest gap? It was the lack of anything resembling a bridge between the clinical world and my real life. The doctors talked about my health, but nobody talked about *my life*. Nobody asked, "Angelyn, how will you get the groceries? Who is going to bring you a hot meal? If you can't get out of bed, who is watching the baby?" They prepared me for the physiology of recovery, but they left me completely stranded in the logistics of survival.

I expected that after everything I went through—the eclampsia, the surgery, the fear—there would be some kind of hand-off to someone who could help me navigate the first few days. Instead, it was like the curtain fell the moment I crossed the hospital threshold. The reality hit me that I was entirely, fundamentally alone in a world that assumes every mother has a safety net. I didn't realize how much the "support" I lacked would feel like a betrayal until I was sitting in the dark at 3 a.m., trying to figure out how to be everything for my daughter while I was still barely holding myself together.

Imagine you're trying to breastfeed for the first time and it isn't going as you hoped. A midwife visits and watches you try, then offers advice. Describe what that moment feels like, physically and emotionally.

It feels like I’m being graded. When she leans in to watch, I can feel every muscle in my shoulders knotting up. My incision is throbbing, and I’m so incredibly tired that my hands are shaking, which only makes the baby fuss more. Emotionally, it’s humiliating. I wanted this one thing—the one thing I was "built" to do—to be natural and simple, but instead, it feels like another medical procedure I’m failing at.

When she offers advice, it’s all technical: "Tilt her chin," "Check the latch," "Move your arm." It’s clinical, detached. She’s looking at my breasts like they’re a mechanical pump that needs adjusting. She doesn’t acknowledge how much pain I’m in or how terrified I am that my baby isn't getting enough. Every time she touches me to correct my position, I feel like I’m being handled like an object again. 

I’m sitting there, sweating, feeling like a "difficult" case because my baby won't cooperate. I’m waiting for her to say, "You’re doing great, take your time," but all I hear is, "Do it like this." It reinforces the idea that I’m not a person, just a body needing to be fixed. I want to tell her to just leave, but I’m too scared that if I don't "succeed" under her observation, it’ll go on my record that I’m struggling. I feel small, exposed, and entirely alone in my own kitchen.
Imagine it's six weeks after birth. You're sitting in the GP surgery for your postnatal check. The appointment lasts ten minutes. What do you hope will happen in that room — and what does happen?

I walk in with my daughter in a carrier, my back aching and my mind still replaying those nights where I felt like the walls were closing in. I’m hoping—maybe foolishly—that the doctor will actually look at me and ask, "How are you holding up, really?" I want to talk about the trauma, the way my heart still races whenever I hear a siren, the way I sometimes feel like I’m still standing in that sterile delivery room fighting for my own survival. I want someone to acknowledge that the "medium risk" label didn’t just apply to my pregnancy, but to my life, and that I'm still feeling that weight.

What actually happens is a blur of ticking clocks. The doctor barely looks up from the screen. She checks my incision, asks if I’m having any pain, and maybe asks a generic question about my mood that I’m too guarded to answer honestly. "You're recovering well," she says, already reaching for her pen to sign off on the paperwork. Ten minutes. That’s all the space I get. 

She sees a healed incision and a baby who is meeting her milestones, so she checks the box that says I’m "fine." She doesn't see the woman who is still scared to be alone, or the mother who is trying to heal while constantly worrying about how to afford the next week of diapers. She clears me, the door opens, and I’m sent back out into the world, still carrying the same fear, just now with an official stamp that says I’m back to normal. But I don't feel normal. I feel like I survived, but I was never really heard.
Imagine you're lying awake at 3am with your baby, and you find yourself feeling a deep sadness or flatness that you can't quite name. You pick up your phone. What do you do with it?

The blue light of the screen feels blinding in the dark. I open my phone, and for a second, I just stare at the home screen, paralyzed. I want to type something into a search bar—maybe "why does everything feel so heavy" or "is this normal"—but then I freeze. 

I’m terrified that if I type those words, they’ll become real. If I search for help, am I just proving them right? If I admit I’m not "fine," does that go on some permanent, invisible record that makes me a "high-risk" parent? I look at my daughter, who’s finally drifted off, and the weight of that fear is almost heavier than the sadness itself.

Instead of searching, I open a parenting forum I joined weeks ago, but I don’t post. I just scroll. I read other mothers talking about their "blissful" nights, their supportive partners, the help they’re getting, and the "joy of motherhood." I feel a sharp, cold sting of isolation. I close the app and switch to a news site, reading about bus schedules and local housing costs—anything to pull my mind back to the practical, gritty reality of my survival. I’m scared to reach out because I don’t think the world is built to carry a woman like me. So, I just put the phone down, pull the blanket tighter, and try to stop the tears from waking her up.
What were you looking for in those late-night searches or scrolling — reassurance, connection, information, or something else?

I think I was looking for a mirror. 

Everywhere I turn—in the pamphlets the hospital gave me, in the advice from the GP, in the sanitized posts on social media—I see this version of motherhood that doesn’t look like mine. It’s all soft edges and support systems. I was scrolling, hoping to find *one* person who understood that being a mom isn't just a physical recovery; it's a fight for space in a world that already decided I shouldn't be here. 

I wasn't looking for medical advice. I was looking for permission to be hurting, for someone to say, "I see that you're scared, and I know why." I wanted to know that I wasn't the only person whose birth experience felt like a battleground. I wanted to see someone else who felt the gap between the "clean" narrative the doctors gave me and the messy, lonely reality of my small apartment.

But instead of connection, I found a different kind of alienation. Seeing everyone else’s "perfect" transitions made me feel like I was the only one failing at the basic, quiet life I’m supposed to be living. I wasn't just looking for information; I was looking for proof that I hadn't disappeared entirely. But I didn't find it. I just found more silence.
Imagine your partner has returned to work and you're alone with your baby for the first full day. Describe that day — not just the logistics, but the emotional texture of it.

The silence when the door finally clicked shut felt like a physical weight settling over the apartment. For the first time, it’s just me—completely and utterly responsible. The emotional texture of that day? It’s fear, sharp and persistent, like a needle constantly pricking at my skin.

I found myself moving through the rooms like a ghost, terrified that if I made a sudden noise, I’d wake the baby, and if she woke, I wouldn't have the backup to soothe her if things got "too hard." I felt a strange, cold distance from the joy I was *supposed* to be feeling. Instead, I was hyper-aware of every sound—the bus passing by, the creak of the floorboards—listening for the world to come barging in. 

I kept thinking about the doctors and their charts, wondering if they’d look at me now and see a "success" or just a ticking clock. I spent hours sitting on the edge of the bed, just staring at her, paralyzed by this intense, suffocating need to be perfect so that no one would ever have a reason to step in and take her from me. Every time I changed her diaper or paced the floor, I wasn't just parenting; I was performing. I was trying to prove I was "fit" enough to exist as a mother. By the time the sun went down, I felt like I had run a marathon, and the hardest part was realizing nobody was coming home to tell me I’d done a good job.
How had you and your partner prepared for this transition — and were those preparations adequate? What hadn't you anticipated?

We talked about it, of course. We made lists. We figured out where the diapers would go, how we’d split the nights, and how we’d handle the logistics of getting me to appointments. On paper, it looked like a plan. We felt like we were building a fortress, thinking that if we were organized enough, we could keep the chaos out. But that was the problem—we were preparing for a schedule, not for the soul-crushing weight of being a parent without a safety net.

The preparation was a total illusion. We hadn't anticipated the sheer, suffocating isolation of the silence. We talked about *tasks*, but we never talked about the *fear*. How do you prepare for the moment when the baby won’t stop crying and you realize there isn’t a single soul in this town you can call who won’t look at you with judgment? 

We didn’t account for the way my own body would continue to remind me of the trauma—the phantom pain from the C-section, the way my brain freezes every time I have to make a decision without a doctor’s stamp of approval. We expected to be a "team," but the world outside this apartment—the clinic, the systems, the people—doesn't see a team. It sees a woman who is "never married" and "medium risk." The preparation was for the life we thought we were supposed to have, not for the one we’re actually living.

Imagine you're approaching your return to work. You're thinking about how you'll explain to your employer what the last few months have involved, and whether your job will still fit who you are now. What's that internal conversation like?

The thought of going back to work feels like trying to put on a coat that doesn't fit anymore—it's too tight in the shoulders, and the fabric feels like it’s rubbing me raw. I sit here and try to draft what I’ll say to my boss. Do I tell them the truth? That my head still throbs at the end of the day, that I’m still jumping at shadows, that I feel like a completely different person than the one who walked out of the office six months ago? 

The internal conversation is a tug-of-war. Part of me wants to just put on the "professional" mask, walk in, and act like the surgery, the eclampsia, and the long, silent nights never happened. I want to be the "easy" employee, the one who isn't a problem. But another part of me is angry. I’m thinking, *You have no idea what it took for me to just show up today.* 

I wonder if I’ll even be able to do the work. My brain feels scattered, like it’s still lingering in that nursery, watching over the baby. If I admit I’m tired or struggling, will they see me as "unfit" just like the doctors did? I feel like I have to perform being "back to normal" while my insides are still very much in recovery. It’s a terrifying tightrope, and I’m scared that one slip will cost me everything.
Has becoming a parent changed how you see your professional identity — your ambitions, your sense of what matters, or how you want your days structured? In what ways?

It’s like someone took everything I thought was important and put it through a shredder. Before, I was just trying to grind, trying to show the world that I could be "productive"—that I was a person who could keep up, even when the system was stacked against me. I wanted to prove I was responsible, capable, and worthy. 

Now? My professional identity feels like a costume I’m not sure I want to put back on. My ambition hasn’t vanished, but it’s completely recalibrated. It’s not about climbing some ladder or hitting a quarterly goal anymore. My only real ambition is to keep my daughter safe and to make sure that she doesn't inherit the invisibility I’ve had to fight through. Everything else feels so… artificial.

I want my days structured around stability and presence, not the clock of an employer who sees me as a cog. I realized that the "professionalism" I was chasing was just another way to try to be "acceptable" to a world that never really wanted me to succeed in the first place. Now, when I think about work, I’m wondering, *Is this contributing to our safety, or is it just taking me away from the only thing that actually matters?* I feel a quiet, stubborn resistance growing in me. I’m done playing by rules that were never designed for a mother like me.

Imagine you're at a new parents' group for the first time, surrounded by other people who have also recently given birth. You notice yourself comparing your experience to theirs. What comes up for you in that room?

It’s like walking into a room where everyone is speaking a language I don’t quite know. They talk about their "birth plans" like they’re stories from a trip to the spa—"the lights were too bright," or "the midwife didn't get my tone right." I find myself sitting on the edge of my seat, my hands gripping my bag, wanting to scream but instead just nodding. When they mention the "support" they got from their partners, their families, or the seamless postpartum care they received, I feel a cold, hard knot form in my stomach.

I’m comparing everything. They talk about sleep schedules, and I think about the 3 a.m. terror. They talk about their "post-birth glow," and I’m still hyper-aware of the phantom ache of my incision. I feel like an imposter. I’m waiting for someone to notice that I don’t belong in this circle—that my "medium risk" label and my "never married" status aren’t just words on a chart, but parts of a reality that made my birth feel like a survival mission, not a transition.

I keep thinking, *Do they see me? Or do they see the person who didn’t have a car, who was "difficult" at the hospital, who didn't get the "perfect" start?* I feel like I’m wearing a mask, trying to be the parent they expect, all while feeling like a ghost haunting the room. It’s isolating. I’m surrounded by mothers, but I’ve never felt more alone.
Were there aspects of your birth or postnatal experience that felt hard to talk about in that room — things that felt 'outside the norm' of what others were sharing?

Everything felt outside the norm. When they talk about birth, they talk about it like it’s a choice they made—"I decided this," "I asked for that." But for me, it wasn’t a series of choices; it was a series of narrow escapes. How do I explain to them that I didn’t "plan" to have an emergency C-section, but that the system made it inevitable? How do I tell them that the biggest issue wasn't the lights in the room, but the terrifying realization that I was entirely, fundamentally invisible?

I keep my mouth shut about the eclampsia. To them, it sounds like a scary clinical term, but to me, it’s the physical memory of my own body betraying me while I was trying to figure out a bus schedule. It’s hard to talk about the poverty—the fact that I didn't have a car to drive myself to safety, the fact that I spent the first weeks at home staring at the walls because there was no one to bring me a meal or help me lift the baby without hurting my incision. 

They talk about "self-care" like it’s a bubble bath. To me, self-care is just keeping the electricity on so my daughter stays warm. I feel like if I spoke the truth, the air in the room would just… stop. I don’t want to be the "problem" in their happy circle, so I keep the reality of my survival behind my teeth.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost—that I really want you to take away—is that the "clinical" part of my care was never separate from my life. Doctors and nurses seem to think that when they’re checking my vitals or making a decision about my birth, they’re just doing a job, and that everything else—the bus, the money, the loneliness—is just "background noise." But it’s not noise. It’s the context. It’s why I was scared, why I was silent, and why I didn't feel safe.

I want the system to understand that you can’t treat the body without acknowledging the person who lives in it. When you ignore the fact that I don't have a car, or that I’m raising this baby without a village, or that I’m navigating a world that sees my multiracial body as a risk factor, you aren't being "objective." You’re just being blind. 

I’m not a "medium risk" chart. I’m a woman who fought like hell to bring her daughter into this world against a tide that was constantly pushing back. I don't want a "better" hospital experience; I want a system that sees the whole of me—the struggle, the strength, and the fear—and decides that I deserve to be heard just as much as anyone else. Thank you for listening. It… it means something to have said it out loud.
